title: Gyromagnetic Quantum Friction in Rayleigh Vorticity Baths
authors: Mamoru Matsuo, Ryotaro Sano, Ai Yamakage, Hiroshi Funaki, Tatsuhiko N. Ikeda
abstract: We identify an intrinsic zero-temperature relaxation channel for near-surface spins gyromagnetically coupled to Rayleigh-wave vorticity. This surface-mode contribution requires no thermal phonons, unlike Raman relaxation, and is fixed by Rayleigh vorticity rather than material-specific $g$-factor modulation. The Rayleigh-vorticity bath is super-Ohmic and evanescent with depth, producing field and depth scalings of spin relaxation. These scalings establish shallow spin sensors and hybrid surface-acoustic-wave spin interfaces as detectors of Rayleigh-wave acoustic quantum friction in solids.
summary: The paper identifies a new relaxation mechanism for spins near a surface called gyromagnetic quantum friction, driven by the vorticity of Rayleigh waves. Unlike thermal Raman relaxation, this process remains active at zero temperature due to zero-point fluctuations. This effect provides a way to use shallow spin sensors to detect acoustic quantum friction in solids.
main_problem: Identifying an intrinsic, zero-temperature relaxation channel for near-surface spins caused by the vorticity of Rayleigh surface acoustic waves.
main_result: The discovery of 'gyromagnetic quantum friction,' a non-zero relaxation rate that persists even at zero temperature due to zero-point vorticity fluctuations, which can be used to detect surface acoustic waves.
method: The authors use the Born-Markov approximation and Fermi's Golden Rule to calculate transition rates, alongside a spectral density analysis of quantized elastic modes.
model_or_system: A single spin-1/2 (such as an NV center or quantum dot) located at a depth d below a traction-free, isotropic elastic surface, coupled to a bath of Rayleigh-wave vorticity and bulk phonons via a Barnett-type interaction.
key_observables: Longitudinal relaxation rate (T1 inverse), transverse coherence time (T2), and frequency renormalization.
important_parameters: Spin depth (d), Larmor frequency (omega_0), Rayleigh-evanescent length (l_R), and the Raman crossover temperature (T_x).
main_assumptions: The medium is an infinite, isotropic, elastic half-space; the interaction is treated in the weak-coupling regime; and the Rayleigh surface contribution is assumed to dominate the bulk contribution for shallow spins.
figures_summary: A visibility map summarizing dominant relaxation regimes based on spin depth and temperature.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the spin-vorticity interaction, develops the quantization of the Rayleigh-wave bath, derives the spectral density and relaxation rates, compares the mechanism to Raman and g-modulation relaxation, and provides a benchmark analysis using diamond parameters.
why_interesting: It identifies a fundamental, temperature-independent decoherence mechanism (quantum friction) driven by vacuum fluctuations of a mechanical bath, which is highly relevant to the design of surface-based spin qubits and quantum sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A near-surface spin-1/2 is located at a depth d below a traction-free surface (z = 0) of an elastic half-space. The spin couples linearly to the local lattice vorticity generated by Rayleigh surface acoustic waves with in-plane wave vector q. A static magnetic field B0 defines the quantization axis, tilted by an angle θB from the surface normal ˆz.
